evidence_quotes.quote: Many game publishers have been getting away by taking money from the consumer by selling video games that are basically ticking timebombs.
they pull the plug and the copy is bricked.
Ubisoft changing their EULA and saying once they take down the servers for a game the user is obliged to destroyer their own copy.
they also took away digital copies of games before from users like The Crew from game libraries on third party platforms.

paragraphs.text: I appreciate all the ideas so far and I think it's certainly needed for a better internet. However I do want to add the Stop Killing Games initiative to the act. Many game publishers have been getting away by taking money from the consumer by selling video games that are basically ticking timebombs. They sell games that can only be played with a server check while some of them have no purpose of having it, they can be played perfectly fine without it but the publisher keeps insisting on having it. Once the publisher decides they don't want to support it anymore they pull the plug and the copy is bricked. Back in the days they were generous enough to provide the tools to allow players to self host if it was an online game without the need for their support, but this is something they've killed off because they can earn more money by selling a new version and basically brick the copy players have. One example I can think of is a video game called Darkspore, it was a game with elements that could be played perfectly fine without an internet but they added systems to make sure it always needed an online check to play online. When they werent happy with the sales they just took it offline and ended support for it. The game however was still sold in video game stores and nowhere on the box does it say it have a warning that it has a limited date of availability. The only warning they give is that it needs online but never do they say they can take the service down anytime. When I got home I was surprised that I couldnt even start the game because they took the service down, it was an absolute waste of 60 euros. It's understandable developers don't want to keep supporting such a thing forever and that's not what most of us ask for, it should be fair that they do however have an end-of-life plan for a product that they sold to players and allow the users to keep playing something they purchased. Simply providing the tools to self host is already sufficient or patch their game to make it work without server checks. Consumers are not asking for endless support, this is and argument often brought up by the publisher but all the consumers want is a consumer friendly end of life plan if they dont want to support their game anymore. This has gotten really bad lately with a publisher like Ubisoft changing their EULA and saying once they take down the servers for a game the user is obliged to destroyer their own copy, they also took away digital copies of games before from users like The Crew from game libraries on third party platforms. Many games have already disappeared forever and as someone who does think games can be an art form I do think it's a shame so many games have been lost now in an unplayable state. I would like to ask to please check out the Stop Killing Games initiative that has gotten over a million signatures at the EU citizen initiative, many people want to keep their purchases working and wish for some regulation on how games are treated by publishers. Please consider adding this too to the Digital Fairness Act.
